water_temp_read.read: Water temperature is in a favorable trout range.

light_pressure_read.read: High pressure and low cloud cover make this fishable but technical.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

caution_notes: Bright/high-pressure conditions may make fish cover-oriented and less forgiving.

generated_text.quick_read: Trout fishing on the Chattahoochee River is good but technical due to bright high-pressure conditions and low cloud cover. Water temperature is ideal for trout activity with recent stocking supporting good catch opportunities.
generated_text.best_window: The day 1 morning is best because low wind, low rain probability, and partial sky cover create favorable conditions for clean drifts and cautious feeding behavior, matching the recommended careful presentations and natural smaller flies.
generated_text.current_conditions: Water temperature is 59.0°F with flow at 771 cfs and a gage height of 1.83 ft. Turbidity is low at 2.3, providing clear water conditions.
generated_text.weather_setup: The sky cover is low averaging 22%, pressure is high at 30.11 inHg with an unknown trend, and winds are light averaging 1.6 mph from the NE. Rain probability is low at 16%, with no expected precipitation.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout will favor riffles, runs, seams, and structure as well as shade, deeper runs, and undercut banks to stay comfortable under bright high pressure. Use clean drifts with smaller natural flies, nymphs, midges, soft hackles, and stocked attractors like egg patterns and squirmy worms. Caution is advised as fish may be cover-oriented and less forgiving due to bright conditions.
